title: The Last Bridge
content:
The concrete groaned.

Liu Yang felt it before he heard it—a vibration traveling up through his boots, through his spine, into the bones of his skull. Three years as a civil engineer had taught him to read a structure's death rattle. This one was screaming.

"Get back!" He shoved the foreman hard enough to send the man stumbling onto wet asphalt. The crane operator was still dangling thirty meters up, frozen against the gray Shanghai sky.

The bridge shuddered again.

Liu Yang's mind did the math automatically. Load distribution: 4,200 tons of wet concrete. Support cables rated for 3,800. Temperature expansion from the afternoon sun had added another 200 tons of stress. Someone had cut corners. Someone always cut corners.

"Zhang!" He pointed at the crane. "Lower the boom, now!"

The crane didn't move. The operator was either frozen or dead.

Liu Yang ran.

His boots slapped against the unfinished deck. Rebar poked through like exposed ribs. The wind off the Huangpu River smelled like diesel and wet stone. He'd walked this bridge a hundred times during inspections, noted the hairline cracks in the support pillars, filed the reports that got buried under three layers of bureaucratic indifference.

Now the bridge was going to file its own report.

The first cable snapped with a sound like a gunshot.

Liu Yang grabbed the ladder and climbed. His arms burned. The crane platform was fifteen meters up. Twelve. Eight. The whole structure swayed beneath him, and he could feel it—the exact moment when elastic deformation became plastic, when the steel gave up being steel and decided to become scrap.

The operator was a kid. Twenty-two, maybe. His name tag said Chen Wei. His face was white.

"Can't move it," the kid stammered. "Controls are dead."

Liu Yang looked down. The bridge deck was splitting along the centerline. A crack like a black lightning bolt racing toward the support pillar.

"Then we jump."

"What?"

Liu Yang grabbed the kid's harness and yanked him toward the edge. The crane tilted. The world tilted. Below them, the river churned gray and hungry.

The bridge collapsed.

Not all at once. That only happened in movies. In real life, it happened in stages—first the center span dropping like a trapdoor, then the support pillars twisting, then the whole eastern section peeling away from the bank like a scab being torn off a wound.

Liu Yang held onto the kid and jumped.

For a moment, he was flying.

Then the concrete hit.

---

Waking up felt like dying in reverse.

Liu Yang's eyes opened. His body felt wrong. Too light. Too clean. The pain was gone—the shattered ribs, the crushed leg, the blood filling his lungs like wet cement. All of it, just gone.

He was lying on a cloud.

Not a metaphor. An actual cloud. It was warm and slightly springy, like memory foam with a 90% cotton content. White mist curled around his fingers. Above him, a sky that looked nothing like Shanghai's smog-gray ceiling stretched endless and impossibly blue.

"Hell of a landing."

Liu Yang sat up.

He was on a bridge.

And it was the worst bridge he'd ever seen.

The structure stretched across an expanse of golden light, connecting two floating islands that shimmered like mother-of-pearl. The design was Chinese classical—arched beams, ornate railings, carved dragons coiling around support pillars. Beautiful, if you didn't know what you were looking at.

Liu Yang knew what he was looking at.

The main arch had a span-to-rise ratio that would make any structural engineer weep. The load-bearing pillars were decorative, not functional—all ornament, no substance. The joints were mortise and tenon without any mechanical reinforcement. And the material—

He stood up and walked to the railing. Touched it. It felt like jade, but lighter. Translucent. Qi-steel, his brain supplied, though he had no idea where that word came from. It was brittle. Beautiful and brittle, like a teacup pretending to be a skyscraper.

"First time?"

Liu Yang turned.

An old man stood at the base of the bridge. He wore flowing white robes embroidered with gold thread. His beard reached his navel. His expression was the tired, patient look of someone who had explained the same thing ten thousand times.

"This is the Bridge of Immortality," the old man said. "You're dead. Welcome to the Heavenly Domain. There's a welcome packet at the registration hall, but I should warn you—the queue is about four hundred years long."

Liu Yang looked back at the bridge.

His eyes traced the load path. From the deck to the arch, from the arch to the abutments, from the abutments to... nothing. The foundations didn't connect to anything solid. They just sort of rested on the cloud below.

"Who built this?"

The old man blinked. "The Jade Emperor, of course. With his own hands. Eight hundred years ago."

"Eight hundred years."

"Yes."

"And in eight hundred years, no one thought to add shear reinforcement?"

The word 'shear' hit the old man like a foreign language. He stared at Liu Yang with the blank incomprehension of a cat watching a magic trick.

"I'm sorry?"

Liu Yang walked to the center of the bridge. He could feel it shifting beneath him—micro-fractures in the jade, stress points where the arch was slowly, inexorably failing. The bridge was dying. Not today, not tomorrow. But soon. In geological terms, anyway.

"Do you have earthquakes here?"

"We have the occasional tantrum from the Dragon King," the old man said slowly. "But the bridge has stood for eight centuries. It's perfectly stable."

"It's not." Liu Yang crouched down and ran his hand along the base of the arch. The jade was warm. Alive, almost. "The compression stress on the lower chord is exceeding the material's yield strength by about forty percent. The arch is slowly deforming. In another fifty years, the apex will drop by half a meter. In a hundred, the whole thing collapses."

Silence.

The old man's face cycled through several expressions—confusion, disbelief, and finally a kind of wary respect.

"You're a builder," he said.

"Engineer. Civil."

"Same thing, here. We don't have many." The old man stroked his beard. "Most souls who arrive spend their first century sightseeing. Or complaining. You're the first one to insult the Jade Emperor's architecture within five minutes of arrival."

"I'm not insulting it. I'm diagnosing it." Liu Yang stood up, brushing dust from his hands. "How long until someone important hears about this?"

"How long until you get your audience with the Jade Emperor?"

Liu Yang had been on the job site too long to miss a negotiation opening when he heard one. He looked at the old man—really looked. The tired eyes. The creased robes. The way he stood like a man who'd been standing in the same spot for a very long time.

"You've been waiting for someone like me," Liu Yang said.

The old man smiled. It was a small, worn-out smile, like a crack in old porcelain.

"Eight hundred years of watching a bridge slowly fail," he said. "Eight hundred years of telling the Emperor that something's wrong, and having him wave it away with a flick of his sleeve. 'It's immortal jade,' he says. 'It'll last forever.' But I can feel it. Every day, a little more. The groaning. The settling. The—

There was a sound like a wine glass being struck.

A hairline crack ran up the base of the arch.

The old man's face went pale. "It's never done that before."

"Not true." Liu Yang walked to the crack and ran his thumb along it. "It's been doing that for years. You just couldn't see it. The fracture started at the microscopic level. Now it's visible."

"What do we do?"

Liu Yang pulled a small notepad from his pocket. He didn't know why he still had it—he was dead, he shouldn't have pockets—but the paper was there, and so was the pen. He started drawing.

"We need to reinforce the arch. Install a secondary support system that redistributes the load. Steel cables, anchored to the abutments. We'll need to calculate the exact tension to avoid overstressing the existing structure."

The old man stared at the scribbled diagram. "Steel? In heaven?"

"You have something better?"

"Qi-steel. It's what everything's made of. Stronger than anything mortal, but..."

"But what?"

"It doesn't like being told what to do."

Liu Yang looked up from his notepad. "The metal has opinions?"

"The metal has qi. And qi has a mind of its own." The old man shrugged. "You'll learn. This isn't your world. The rules are different here."

Liu Yang looked at the bridge. At the hairline crack. At the golden light streaming between the floating islands.

Different rules. Fine.

Physics was physics. Whether it ran on gravity or qi, a load was a load. Stress was stress. Failure was failure.

He closed his notepad.

"Take me to the Jade Emperor."

The old man hesitated. "He doesn't like being interrupted during meditation."

"Tell him his bridge is falling down."

"He'll say it's not."

"Then I'll show him the math."

The old man studied him for a long moment. Then he laughed—a dry, rasping sound, like leaves being crushed.

"You know," he said, "I think you might actually survive here."

Liu Yang didn't answer.

He was already calculating.

The arch had a span of roughly two hundred meters. The crack was at the quarter-span point, which meant the maximum bending moment was concentrated there. If the crack propagated another thirty centimeters, the entire eastern half would lose structural integrity.

He had maybe a week. Possibly less.

And he needed to find a pencil.

"One more thing," Liu Yang said, as they walked toward the island. "What's your name?"

The old man straightened his robes. "I am Grand Secretary Xu, Keeper of the Heavenly Records, First Minister to the Jade Emperor's Left Hand."

"That's a mouthful."

"You may call me Xu."

"Liu Yang."

"I know." Xu smiled. "I've been expecting you for eight hundred years. I just didn't know it until now."

They walked into the golden light, and behind them, the Bridge of Immortality groaned.

title: The Jade Emperor's Plumbing Problem
content:
The cloud bridge collapsed three times before breakfast.

Liu Yang stood at the edge of the Eastern Sky Plaza, watching celestial workers scramble to catch falling pavilions. Golden tiles tumbled through the void, spinning like autumn leaves before disappearing into the mist below. Nobody seemed concerned.

"Third time this week," said a passing official, adjusting his hat. "The Emperor says it's bad qi flow."

Liu Yang pulled out his notebook. The numbers didn't lie. The bridge's load-bearing capacity had failed at exactly 2.3 times its rated stress. He'd calculated it during his first night in heaven, lying on a cloud that felt suspiciously like memory foam but smelled of incense and ozone.

"The problem isn't qi," he said.

The official stopped. "What?"

"Your beams are undersized. The distributed load exceeds the tensile strength of the celestial jade by forty percent." Liu Yang tapped his pencil against the notebook. "And your foundation is floating on compressed mist instead of solid bedrock."

The official stared at him like he'd spoken a foreign language. Which, technically, he had. The man's eyes were the color of amber, pupils shifting slowly like liquid gold. Immortal. Probably been alive since the Han Dynasty.

"You're the mortal," the official said. "The one who fell through the sky."

"Fell implies accident. I was pushed."

"By a bridge."

"Same thing."

Liu Yang had learned three things in his first twenty-four hours in the Heavenly Domain. One: the gods had no concept of structural engineering. Two: they solved every problem with more qi, which was like trying to fix a leaking pipe by turning up the water pressure. Three: the Jade Emperor had been trying to seal a leak in the Southern Qi Pipeline since the Tang Dynasty.

Eight hundred years.

Eight hundred years of the same leak, and nobody had thought to check if the pipe was cracked instead of just loosely connected.

The official, whose name turned out to be Feng Bo, led him through the Eastern Sky Plaza. The architecture was spectacular in the way only something built by beings with infinite time and zero budget could be. Pagodas stacked at precarious angles. Bridges that curved like silk ribbons. Stairs that led to platforms that led to more stairs that led to nowhere.

"Where are we going?" Liu Yang asked.

"The Emperor demands your presence."

"Why?"

Feng Bo's golden eyes flickered. "Because the Southern Qi Pipeline just exploded."

---

The Jade Emperor's throne room looked like someone had tried to build the Forbidden City inside a hurricane.

Columns of jade and gold spiraled upward into a ceiling that wasn't there. The walls were made of flowing water, or maybe light, or maybe both. At the center sat a man who looked exactly like every painting Liu Yang had ever seen—except for the expression on his face, which was the universal look of someone who'd been dealing with the same maintenance issue since before the printing press was invented.

"You're the engineer," the Emperor said. It wasn't a question.

"Yes."

"My ministers tell me you spoke heresy about our bridges."

"I told them their beams were undersized."

"And now my pipeline has exploded." The Emperor leaned forward. His robes rustled like thunder. "Coincidence?"

"Bad design isn't a coincidence. It's a pattern."

Someone gasped. Liu Yang didn't care. He'd spent his entire professional life watching people die because someone had cut corners on rebar spacing or used cheap concrete. Heaven wasn't different. It just had better special effects.

The Emperor's expression shifted. The divine anger faded into something that looked almost like curiosity.

"Show me."

---

The Southern Qi Pipeline ran through the heart of the Heavenly Domain.

Liu Yang had expected something impressive. Instead, he found a network of pipes that looked like they'd been assembled by someone who'd never seen a pipe before. Sections connected at random angles. Valves that didn't align with the flow direction. A pressure gauge that had been painted over with gold leaf.

"It's beautiful," he said.

The attending celestial engineer—a woman named Xian Li who'd been in charge of the pipeline for three centuries—puffed up with pride.

"Thank you. I painted the gauge myself."

"That's not a compliment."

Her face fell.

Liu Yang walked to the source of the explosion. The pipe had ruptured along a seam, sending qi bleeding into the void. The immortals had surrounded it with containment crystals, which were doing nothing because the pressure was still building behind the crack.

"Every three hundred years," Xian Li said, "the Emperor orders a new seal. I've applied seventeen seals myself."

"Seventeen."

"Yes."

"And you never thought to replace the pipe?"

"The pipe is celestial jade. It's eternal."

Liu Yang pulled out his calculator. The numbers took thirty seconds to run. He'd done the same math a thousand times on Earth, calculating stress points for bridges, dams, and high-rises. The physics didn't change just because the materials were magical.

"The pipe isn't cracked," he said. "It's fatigued."

"Impossible."

"Celestial jade is brittle under cyclic loading. Every time you seal the leak, the pressure builds until it splits the seam again. You've been putting bandaids on a broken bone." He pointed at the rupture. "The entire section needs to be replaced. And the new section needs expansion joints to handle thermal stress from qi flow."

Xian Li looked like he'd just suggested demolishing the Temple of Heaven. "That would take decades. The materials alone—"

"How long does it take to grow celestial jade?"

"Six months. Minimum."

"Then we start growing it today. In the meantime, I can design a temporary pressure relief system that will prevent further ruptures."

The crowd of celestial officials murmured. Liu Yang spotted Feng Bo in the back, watching with an expression that was impossible to read. The man's amber eyes reflected the glow of the leaking qi.

"You're proposing to redesign heaven," Feng Bo said.

"I'm proposing to fix your plumbing."

"The difference being?"

Liu Yang turned back to the pipeline. The qi was still bleeding into the void, staining the clouds with streaks of blue light. Beautiful, in its way. Deadly, if it kept building.

"The difference," he said, "is that I actually know what I'm doing."

---

The work began at midnight.

Heaven didn't have midnight, technically—the light never fully faded, just shifted to a softer gold—but Liu Yang had always worked best in the hours between dark and dawn. The celestial construction site was empty, the workers having retreated to their cloud-palaces for meditation or whatever immortals did when they weren't failing at basic engineering.

He laid out his plans on a sheet of jade-paper. The calculations were simple, once you understood the material properties. Celestial jade had a compressive strength of approximately 800 megapascals, which was better than concrete but worse than steel. Its tensile strength was abysmal. Whoever had designed the original pipeline had assumed the material was indestructible, which was the single most dangerous assumption in all of engineering.

"Working late."

Liu Yang didn't look up. He'd heard the footsteps—or rather, the lack of footsteps. Immortals moved like smoke.

"Someone has to."

Feng Bo stepped into the light. He'd shed his official robes for something simpler, a gray tunic that made him look almost mortal.

"The Emperor is impressed."

"He should be. I'm fixing an eight-hundred-year-old problem."

"The Emperor has been fixing it for eight hundred years."

"No. He's been failing to fix it for eight hundred years. There's a difference."

Feng Bo was silent for a long moment. Then he sat down on the cloud-floor, cross-legged, like he had all the time in the world. Which he did.

"Where did you come from, Liu Yang?"

"Earth."

"That's not what I mean."

Liu Yang looked up from his designs. Feng Bo's golden eyes were fixed on him, and there was something in them that hadn't been there before. Something sharp.

"I mean," Feng Bo said, "why you? The Heavens have received souls from Earth for millennia. Farmers. Warriors. Poets. Never an engineer."

"Maybe the universe finally got tired of watching you build things wrong."

Feng Bo smiled. It wasn't a friendly smile.

"We'll see how long you last."

---

The next morning, Liu Yang woke to find his designs had been stolen.

He searched his quarters—a modest cloud-chamber with a bed that floated six inches off the ground—but the jade-paper was gone. Every calculation, every drawing, every note he'd made about the pipeline's redesign.

Vanished.

He found Xian Li at the construction site, staring at the leaking pipe with an expression of deep satisfaction.

"Good news," she said. "The Emperor has ordered a new seal."

"What?"

She held up her hand. In her palm sat a fresh seal of celestial jade, perfectly carved, glowing with qi.

"The materials are ready. I'll have the rupture sealed by sunset."

Liu Yang's blood went cold. "That's a temporary fix. It'll fail again."

"The Emperor's orders."

"The Emperor doesn't understand the problem."

Xian Li's smile didn't waver. "The Emperor understands everything."

Liu Yang turned and ran.

---

The throne room was empty.

No guards. No ministers. Just the Jade Emperor, sitting on his throne, reading a scroll that glowed with golden characters.

"Your Majesty."

The Emperor didn't look up. "Liu Yang. I heard you were upset about the seal."

"Your engineers are going to make the problem worse."

"They've been solving it for centuries."

"No. They've been hiding it. Every time you seal a leak, you're building pressure somewhere else. The next rupture will be bigger. And the one after that. Until the entire pipeline fails."

The Emperor set down his scroll. His eyes were ancient. Tired.

"And what would you have me do?"

"Shut down the pipeline. Replace the damaged section. Install expansion joints. It'll take six months, but when it's done, the system will work."

"Six months without qi flow."

"Better than an explosion that destroys half the Eastern Sky."

The Emperor was silent. The golden light flickered around him, casting shadows that moved like living things.

"You're asking me to admit that my engineers have been wrong for eight centuries."

"I'm asking you to admit that they don't know what they're doing."

"And if I refuse?"

Liu Yang met his gaze. "Then you'll still have a leaking pipe in another eight hundred years."

The Emperor laughed. It was a hollow sound, like a bell cracking.

"Your designs were stolen."

"I know."

"I have them."

Liu Yang's heart stopped.

The Emperor reached into his sleeve and pulled out a roll of jade-paper. Liu Yang's notes. His calculations. His drawings.

"You're right," the Emperor said. "The pipeline is going to fail. I've known for four hundred years."

"Then why—"

"Because admitting the problem means admitting that heaven is not perfect." The Emperor's voice dropped to a whisper. "And if heaven is not perfect, then what is?"

The jade-paper glowed in his hands.

"I have a new project for you, Liu Yang."

"What kind of project?"

The Emperor smiled. It was the smile of a man who had been waiting a very long time for someone to ask the right question.

"The kind that will get you killed."

title: The God Who Forgot His Own Name
content:
The cloud bridge across the Western Sky Gap had stood for twelve thousand years.

Liu Yang stood on it for exactly twelve seconds before he wanted to cry.

"This is held together by spit and prayer," he muttered, kneeling to run his fingers along the fractured surface. The clouds beneath his touch were solid enough—condensed qi, he'd learned, compressed to the density of granite. But the structural joints? A disaster. Cracks spiderwebbed outward from every connection point. Some gaps were wide enough to stick his entire hand through.

Behind him, a cough.

He turned. The old maintenance immortal—the one who'd been fixing leaks for eight centuries—stood with his arms crossed. Gray robes. Gray beard. Gray expression.

"You're the mortal," the old man said.

"Liu Yang."

"I heard you insulted my work."

Liu Yang stood. "I said it was held together by spit and prayer."

The old man's eye twitched. "That spit and prayer has held heaven together since before your ancestors learned to walk upright."

"Your rebar is rusting." Liu Yang pointed at a dark streak running through the cloudstone. "That's not rust. That's decay. Qi corrosion. The material is breaking down at the molecular—at the spiritual level. When did you last reinforce this?"

"Eight hundred years ago. Before that, another six hundred."

"You've been maintaining a structure that's due for complete replacement for eight centuries?"

The old man's jaw tightened. "The Jade Emperor assigns tasks. I perform them."

"And you've never asked for more resources? Better materials?"

"One does not question the Jade Emperor."

Liu Yang looked at the bridge again. Looked at the drop beneath it—an endless white void that led to nothing mortal minds could comprehend. Looked at the old immortal whose hands shook slightly, whose eyes held the particular exhaustion of someone who'd been fighting a losing battle for longer than most civilizations had existed.

"Show me your workshop," Liu Yang said.

---

The workshop was a cavern carved into a floating mountain. Inside: tools that looked like they hadn't changed in ten thousand years. Hammers of jade. Chisels of celestial bronze. A forge that burned with pale blue flame—the same flame, the old man explained, that had been lit by the first architects of heaven.

No blueprints. No calculations. No measurements.

"How do you know how much material you need?" Liu Yang asked.

"I know."

"How do you know the bridge can hold weight?"

"I know."

"How do you know—"

"I know!" The old man slammed his fist on a workbench. The entire cavern trembled. Dust fell from the ceiling. "I have maintained the Western Sky Bridge for eight hundred years. Before me, my master maintained it for six hundred. Before him, his master for nine hundred. We know. We have always known."

Liu Yang waited.

The old man's shoulders dropped. "But the cracks get worse. Every century, they grow faster. No matter how much qi I pour into the seal, no matter how carefully I patch—" He stopped. Swallowed. "We are losing the bridge."

"When did you first notice the acceleration?"

"Three hundred years ago."

"And you told no one?"

"Who would I tell?" The old man's voice cracked. "The Jade Emperor does not want to hear that heaven is failing. The other immortals do not want to see it. They look at the bridge and see what it was, not what it is. I am the only one who sees the truth."

Liu Yang picked up a broken chunk of cloudstone from the workbench. Examined the fracture pattern. The internal structure. The way the qi had crystallized over millennia, then begun to decay.

"Your problem isn't maintenance," he said.

"It isn't?"

"It's design. This bridge was built to last a thousand years. Maybe two. Someone extended that timeline by pouring more and more qi into it, but you're fighting entropy. You can't win that fight with brute force."

The old man stared at him. "Then how do you win?"

"You rebuild."

The silence stretched. Somewhere in the distance, a celestial bell chimed. The sound carried through the clouds like ripples in water.

"Rebuild," the old man repeated. "You want me to... tear down the bridge?"

"I want you to build a new one. Better materials. Better design. Something that will last ten thousand years instead of one."

"I don't know how."

"I do."

The old man looked at him. Really looked. Liu Yang saw the calculation behind those ancient eyes—the weighing of a mortal's confidence against twelve thousand years of tradition.

"You're serious," the old man said.

"I'm an engineer. It's what I do."

"Engineer." The old man tested the word like it was a foreign fruit. "What does that mean, exactly?"

Liu Yang pulled out his calculator. The old man stared at the glowing numbers.

"What is that?"

"Magic that actually works."

---

They worked for three days.

Liu Yang taught the old man—whose name, he finally learned, was Immortal Hao—the basics of structural load calculation. Showed him how to measure the stress on each section of the bridge. How to calculate the optimal thickness of cloudstone. How to identify weak points before they failed.

Immortal Hao learned fast. He'd been doing this work for centuries by instinct. Now he had theory to match his intuition, and the results were immediate.

"Here," Hao said, pointing at a section of the bridge they'd marked for repair. "If I reinforce the base by three hand-widths and curve the support beam at thirty degrees..."

"The lateral load distribution would improve by twenty percent."

"Yes! Exactly!" Hao's face lit up. "I've been doing this for years, but I never knew why it worked. I just knew it did."

"That's intuition. It's valuable. But it's not reliable."

They worked through the night. Hao's apprentices—young immortals who'd been doing this work for mere centuries—gathered to watch. At first they were skeptical. A mortal, teaching heavenly architects? Preposterous.

Then Liu Yang showed them how to calculate the exact qi density needed for a perfect seal. No more guesswork. No more wasted energy. His formula worked every time.

By the third day, they had a plan. The new bridge would use a hybrid structure: cloudstone reinforced with qi-steel lattice, supported by graduated arches that distributed weight more efficiently. The design would last five thousand years minimum, possibly longer with basic maintenance.

"Five thousand years," Immortal Hao whispered. "I could train five generations of apprentices in that time."

"Or you could build something that doesn't need constant repair."

Hao laughed. It was the first time Liu Yang had heard him laugh. "You're going to put me out of work."

"You're going to have better work."

---

On the fourth day, the Jade Emperor summoned them.

Liu Yang had seen the throne room before, but only in passing. Now he stood in its center, under the gaze of a hundred immortals arranged in precise ranks. The ceiling stretched into infinity. The floor reflected their faces like polished obsidian.

The Jade Emperor sat on a throne of solid jade. He looked exactly as Liu Yang remembered: ageless, composed, with eyes that held the weight of ten thousand years.

"I hear you have been busy, mortal."

"I've been working, Your Excellency."

"You've been teaching." The Emperor's voice was flat. "Without permission. Without authorization. You've been spreading knowledge that was never meant for mortal minds."

Liu Yang's stomach tightened. "I was solving a problem."

"The problem of the Western Sky Bridge."

"Yes."

"And you believe you've solved it?"

"I believe I've designed a better solution than the one currently in use."

A murmur ran through the immortals. The Jade Emperor raised one hand. Silence.

"Immortal Hao," the Emperor said. "Step forward."

Hao moved to stand beside Liu Yang. His hands were clasped in front of him, his head bowed.

"Tell me," the Emperor said. "Has this mortal taught you anything of value?"

Hao hesitated. "He has taught me many things, Your Excellency."

"Such as?"

"How to calculate stress loads. How to optimize qi distribution. How to design structures that will last—" Hao stopped. "That will last longer than our current methods."

The Emperor's expression didn't change. "And you believe these methods are superior to the traditions passed down by your ancestors?"

Another hesitation. This one longer.

"I believe," Hao said slowly, "that tradition and innovation can coexist."

"Can they?" The Emperor leaned forward. "Or does innovation destroy tradition? Does the new always consume the old?"

Liu Yang stepped forward. "Your Excellency, I don't mean to destroy anything. I mean to build something better."

"You are a mortal. You have been in heaven for less than a month. And you believe you can improve upon the work of beings who have existed since before your world was formed."

"I believe that good ideas can come from anywhere."

The Emperor stared at him. The silence stretched like a blade being drawn.

Then the Emperor smiled.

It was not a warm smile.

"You are bold, Liu Yang. I admire boldness." He stood. The immortals bowed. "But boldness without wisdom is dangerous. You have shown that you can improve one bridge. But heaven is not one bridge. Heaven is a million structures, each more complex than the last. Do you truly believe your mortal engineering can fix all of them?"

Liu Yang met his gaze. "I believe I can try."

"Then prove it."

The Emperor gestured. An immortal stepped forward, carrying a scroll. He unfurled it on the floor. Liu Yang looked down.

It was a map. A map of heaven. And on that map, marked in red ink, were a thousand points of failure.

"The Celestial Foundation," the Emperor said, "is collapsing."

Liu Yang's blood went cold.

"We have known for six hundred years. The very bedrock of heaven is cracking. The qi pipelines are rupturing. The Cloud Palace is sinking into the abyss." The Emperor's voice was calm, measured, terrifying. "We have tried everything. Every ritual. Every prayer. Every sacrifice. Nothing works."

He stepped down from his throne. Walked toward Liu Yang. Stood so close that Liu Yang could see the exhaustion hidden behind those ancient eyes.

"You claim to be an engineer. You claim to have solutions." The Emperor's voice dropped to a whisper that carried through the entire hall. "Then fix it. Fix the foundation of heaven. And if you fail—"

He didn't finish the sentence.

He didn't need to.

Liu Yang looked at the map. At the thousand points of failure. At a problem that made the Western Sky Bridge look like a child's puzzle.

And in the corner of his eye, he saw Immortal Hao's face.

Hao already knew.

Everyone already knew.

They'd been waiting for someone to solve the unsolvable.

They'd been waiting for someone who could do what no immortal had done in six hundred years.

They'd been waiting for him.

Liu Yang looked up at the Jade Emperor. Opened his mouth to speak.

And the ceiling collapsed.

title: The Pipeline That Held a Dynasty
content:
The Heavenly Domain's central plaza looked like a construction site after a monsoon.

Liu Yang stood ankle-deep in something that shimmered like liquid starlight but smelled like a blocked sewer. His steel-toed boots—salvaged from the maintenance shed he'd found on day three—were already corroding at the seams. Three days of work, and all he had to show for it was a schematic that looked like a spiderweb drawn by a drunk, a pile of qi-steel beams that kept trying to float away, and a headache that pulsed behind his left eye like a second heartbeat.

"This is impossible," he muttered.

Old Man Feng appeared beside him, carrying a teapot that steamed with something brown and vaguely medicinal. The celestial carpenter had become Liu Yang's unofficial shadow over the past week—part translator, part supplier, part skeptic who refused to admit he was impressed.

"The pipeline runs through seventeen cloud formations," Feng said, pouring tea into a cup that materialized from his sleeve. "Each formation has different density. Different pressure. Different—"

"I know." Liu Yang tapped his calculator. The numbers didn't lie. The qi flow through the Heavenly Domain was designed like a garden hose—straight lines, sharp turns, no consideration for pressure drop or structural load. The immortals had been patching leaks for centuries instead of redesigning the entire system.

And the leaks were getting worse.

Yesterday, a maintenance worker had nearly fallen through a cloud floor that had been weakened by qi erosion. The worker didn't care—he was immortal, after all—but Liu Yang had grabbed his arm and pulled him back. The look on the man's face wasn't gratitude. It was confusion. Like no one had ever bothered to save him before.

"That's the problem," Liu Yang said, taking the tea. He didn't drink it. "You're all infinite. So nothing matters. You patch a leak, it breaks again, you patch it again. For eight hundred years."

Feng's eyes narrowed. "You say this like it's a flaw."

"It's a design failure."

The old man laughed, but there was no humor in it. "You've been here one week, Engineer. You don't know what you're talking about."

"Then show me the main distribution node."

Feng's teacup stopped halfway to his lips.

"Nowhere," Feng said. Too quickly.

"That's what I thought." Liu Yang folded his schematic and tucked it into his pocket. "I need to see where the qi comes from. The source. Every pipeline, every leak, every repair order for the last century—I need access."

"You're asking to see the Celestial Vault."

"Is that what you call the filing room?"

Feng set down his tea. The steam curled upward, forming shapes that looked almost like characters before dissolving. "The Vault is not a filing room. It's the heart of the Heavenly Domain. The Jade Emperor's private archive. No one enters without his permission."

"Then get me his permission."

"You don't ask for things here, Engineer. You wait. You cultivate patience. You—"

"I don't have eight hundred years."

Feng's jaw tightened. For a moment, Liu Yang thought the old man might strike him. Instead, Feng reached into his robe and pulled out a jade token, green as spring leaves, etched with characters that glowed faintly.

"I was the chief engineer before you," Feng said quietly. "Three hundred years ago. I designed the current pipeline system."

Liu Yang stared at him.

"You know it's broken," Feng continued. "But you don't know why. You think it's old age. Poor maintenance. Carelessness." He pressed the token into Liu Yang's hand. "It's none of those things. It was built to fail."

The token was warm. Alive. It pulsed with a rhythm that matched Liu Yang's own heartbeat.

"Who built it?" Liu Yang asked.

Feng looked over his shoulder. The plaza was empty except for a few maintenance workers hauling qi-steel beams across the cloud bridge. No one was watching. But Feng lowered his voice anyway.

"The previous Jade Emperor. The one who disappeared."

"He disappeared?"

"Vanished. No trace. No explanation. One day he was here, the next—" Feng snapped his fingers. "Gone. And the system he left behind? It's been corroding from the inside ever since."

Liu Yang turned the token over in his hands. The characters shifted, rearranging themselves into a map. Pathways. Junctions. A central point marked with a symbol he didn't recognize.

"What's that?" he asked, pointing.

Feng's face went pale. He grabbed Liu Yang's wrist, almost crushing it. "Don't go there. Don't even think about it."

"Why?"

"Because that's where the first emperor went. And whatever took him is still waiting."

---

The Celestial Vault wasn't a vault.

It was a library that stretched into infinity. Shelves made of jade and cloud-stone rose so high Liu Yang couldn't see the ceiling. Scrolls floated between them like fish in a slow current, brushing against his shoulders as he walked. The air smelled of old paper, ozone, and something else—something metallic, like blood.

Feng had refused to come with him. "I've given you the key," he'd said. "The rest is your choice."

So Liu Yang had chosen.

He walked for what felt like hours, following the map that pulsed in his palm. The token guided him through corridors that shifted and rearranged themselves, through rooms filled with artifacts that hummed with power, past doors that whispered his name in voices he didn't recognize.

He didn't stop. He didn't look back.

The central chamber was smaller than he'd expected. A circular room, no more than twenty feet across, with a single pedestal in the center. On the pedestal sat a scroll, bound with red silk.

And on the floor around the pedestal, someone had drawn a circle of characters. Hundreds of them. Thousands. They spiraled outward in patterns that hurt to look at, like staring into the sun.

Liu Yang stepped inside.

The characters flared. Light flooded the room, so bright he had to shield his eyes. The scroll on the pedestal unrolled by itself, revealing lines of text that glowed like molten gold.

He read the first line.

*To the one who finds this: you have three days.*

His blood went cold.

*The pipeline you seek to repair is not broken. It is a cage. Every leak you fix tightens the chains. Every patch you apply strengthens the prison. The qi that flows through the Heavenly Domain is not power. It is a leash.*

Below the text, a diagram. The pipeline system he'd been studying for three days suddenly made sense—but not as infrastructure. As a binding array. Each pipe, each junction, each maintenance hatch formed a symbol that looped back on itself, trapping something in the center.

Something at the node marked on Feng's map.

*The previous Jade Emperor built this to contain a creature he could not kill. The creature has been feeding on the qi of the Heavenly Domain for eight hundred years. It grows stronger with every repair. It grows hungrier with every patch.*

*You are the engineer who will either save this realm or doom it.*

*Choose wisely.*

Liu Yang's hand trembled. The token in his palm went cold.

Behind him, the door to the chamber slammed shut.

The characters on the floor began to move, crawling toward his feet like living things.

And a voice—low, ancient, amused—spoke from the shadows.

"Finally. Someone who knows how to read."

Liu Yang turned.

The creature sitting in the corner of the room had been watching him the entire time.

title: The Furnace That Wouldn't Hold
content:
The morning light in the Heavenly Domain had a texture Liu Yang didn't trust.

It poured through the windows of his workshop like honey — thick, golden, and wrong. Back on Earth, light scattered. Particles hit the atmosphere, bounced around, filled a room evenly. Here, it pooled. It settled in corners like sediment. The physics of it made his teeth ache.

He'd been awake for three days.

The Immortal Smith, a man named Feng Bo who looked seventy but claimed to be twelve thousand years old, had delivered the furnace specifications on scrolls that smelled of sulfur and regret. The drawings were beautiful. Ink brush strokes danced across rice paper, showing a cauldron of impossible elegance, its surface etched with qi-conduction arrays that curved like living vines.

The structural engineering was a disaster.

"This support beam," Liu Yang said, tapping the scroll with his pen. "It's decorative. It does nothing."

Feng Bo's beard twitched. "It channels celestial fire through the heartstone."

"The heartstone's here." Liu Yang drew a circle on the paper. "The beam terminates three hand-widths away. The fire has to jump across a gap that weakens the thermal transfer by—" he did the math in his head, "—roughly forty-seven percent."

"Forty-seven percent?"

"Give or take." Liu Yang pulled out his calculator. The plastic casing had started to warp from exposure to ambient qi. He'd need to reinforce it soon. "And the base plate is too thin. The pressure differential between the molten qi-steel and the cooling chamber will crack it within a hundred cycles. Maybe fifty."

Feng Bo stared at him.

Then he laughed.

"Young man," the smith said, wiping a tear from his eye. "I have forged the weapons of three Celestial Wars. I have tempered blades that cut through fate itself. Do you think I do not understand my own furnace?"

"I think," Liu Yang said, "that you understand fire and metal and qi. I think you don't understand stress tolerances and thermal expansion coefficients. Those are different things."

The laughter stopped.

The silence that followed had weight. It pressed against Liu Yang's ears, made his skin prickle. Feng Bo's eyes narrowed, and for a moment, Liu Yang saw something ancient and dangerous looking out through those wrinkled lids.

But then the old man sighed.

"Show me."

---

They rebuilt the furnace together.

Liu Yang drew up new specifications on graph paper he'd managed to synthesize from cloud fiber and ink berries. The equations were the same ones he'd used on Earth — basic thermodynamics, material science, stress analysis. The numbers just meant different things now. Tensile strength in qi-units instead of megapascals. Thermal conductivity measured in spirit-flow rather than watts per meter-kelvin.

The principles held.

Feng Bo watched over his shoulder, grunting occasionally. He didn't ask questions. Just watched. When Liu Yang finished the calculations for the support beam relocation, the old smith picked up his hammer and started breaking down the existing furnace.

"My ancestors built this design," Feng Bo said, hammer ringing against stone. "For twelve thousand years, we have passed it down. Father to son. Master to apprentice. No one questioned it."

"Maybe you should have."

Feng Bo stopped. Turned. His face was unreadable.

"Maybe you're right."

They worked for three days. The other immortals came to watch. Word had spread — the mortal engineer was rebuilding the Grand Furnace. They stood in the doorway of the forge, their robes rustling, their ancient eyes tracking Liu Yang's every move.

He ignored them.

On the second day, a problem emerged.

The qi-conduction arrays were etched into the furnace walls in a spiral pattern. Beautiful. Elegant. Completely inefficient for uniform heat distribution. Liu Yang redrew them as a grid system, each node connected by parallel channels that would distribute thermal load evenly.

Feng Bo looked at the new pattern.

"This is ugly."

"It works."

"It's a grid."

"Yes."

Feng Bo picked up his etching tool. He carved the first line into the furnace wall. The immortals in the doorway gasped.

Liu Yang didn't gasp. He just checked his measurements and told Feng Bo to deepen the channels by half a finger-width.

On the third day, they tested it.

Feng Bo fed the furnace with heartstone — chunks of crystallized celestial fire that pulsed with trapped starlight. Liu Yang had measured their energy density at roughly the equivalent of a small nuclear reactor. Per stone.

The furnace hummed.

Heat built. The temperature rose evenly across the chamber, the grid pattern distributing thermal energy exactly as the calculations predicted. Feng Bo's face went through a journey — skepticism to surprise to something that looked almost like joy.

"It's holding," he whispered.

"It's supposed to."

Feng Bo threw in more heartstone. The furnace roared. Steam vented from the pressure-release valves Liu Yang had added, pipes that bled off excess qi like safety vents on an industrial boiler. The temperature hit optimal forging range and stayed there. Rock steady. Perfect.

"We can start the first batch," Liu Yang said.

He reached for the control panel he'd built — a series of levers and dials that regulated qi flow, temperature, pressure. Simple analog systems. Nothing that could fail if the power fluctuated.

His hand stopped.

Something was wrong.

The numbers on his gauges were drifting. Not much. A degree here, a half-degree there. Within normal variance. But the pattern was wrong. They weren't fluctuating randomly. They were oscillating. A sine wave moving through the system, growing wider with each cycle.

"Shut it down," Liu Yang said.

"What?"

"The furnace. Shut it down. Now."

Feng Bo grabbed the main lever and pulled. It didn't move. The heat had warped the mechanism, swelling the metal in its housing. Liu Yang threw himself at the emergency release — a valve he'd designed specifically for this failure mode — and cranked it open.

Steam exploded from the vents.

The furnace screamed.

It was a sound Liu Yang had heard before. On a bridge in Henan province, three years ago, when a support cable had snapped and the whole structure had started to sing. A warning frequency. Metal telling you it was about to die.

He grabbed Feng Bo's arm.

"Run."

They made it to the doorway. The furnace didn't explode — it ruptured. A split appeared in the side wall, exactly where the old support beam had been. The original design's flaw, the gap Liu Yang had identified but not fully corrected. The thermal stress had found the weak point.

The split widened.

Qi-steel slag poured out, molten and white-hot, eating through the stone floor. The furnace walls buckled. The roof sagged.

And then, just as suddenly, it stopped.

The slag solidified. The furnace went dark. The immortals in the doorway stared at the wreckage, their faces blank with shock.

Liu Yang's hands were shaking.

He looked at the ruined furnace. At the calculations still pinned to his workbench. At the smoke rising from a structure he'd designed, built, and watched fail.

Twelve thousand years of tradition.

Three days of his math.

He'd missed something.

"Liu Yang."

Feng Bo's voice was quiet. Not angry. Something worse than angry.

"You said the design was fixed."

"I thought it was."

"You thought."

The old smith turned away. His shoulders were set, rigid. When he spoke again, his voice carried to every immortal in the forge.

"The mortal has broken the Grand Furnace. Three thousand heartstone ingots, melted into slag. A structure that has served the Heavenly Domain since before the First Emperor ruled the mortal realm."

He faced Liu Yang.

"Destroyed."

Liu Yang opened his mouth to explain. To talk about thermal oscillation, about the sine wave pattern, about the flaw he'd missed but could still fix.

He never got the chance.

The doors of the forge slammed open. A figure stood in the entrance — tall, armored, radiating authority like heat from a fire. The Celestial Inspector. The woman who had first summoned him to the Heavenly Domain.

Her face was stone.

"Liu Yang of Earth. By order of the Jade Emperor, you are charged with sabotage against the Heavenly Domain. The punishment is dissolution."

She raised her hand.

And the world went white.
